title: Why Rezolve AI Stock Soared 21% Higher Today
description: Its shareholders approved a new stock repurchase program.

title: IMDEA Materials Institute develops AI model to predict fire resistance of epoxy resins
description: Trained on 510 samples, a machine-learning model predicts epoxy resin fire resistance. It replaces slow physical testing by forecasting flammability metrics digitally.

title: Pentagon, industry leaders say speed, scale and AI will define future defense edge
description: As the Pentagon pushes to overhaul its weapons procurement process, senior Defense Department officials and industry executives said Tuesday the next competitive advantage may depend less on designing the most sophisticated systems than on building them faster, at greater scale and with manufacturing processes increasingly driven by digital engineering and artificial intelligence. Speaking at a Center for Strategic and International Studies event on defense industrial capacity, Pentagon acquisition chief Michael Duffey repeatedly returned to a single theme: scale. "It is all about scale," he said, arguing DOD must capitalize on both a deteriorating global security environment and an unprecedented influx of private capital into the defense industrial base by creating conditions that encourage companies to invest ahead of government demand. Industry leaders echoed that message, saying the Pentagon's acquisition reforms are beginning to change how companies think about manufacturing capacity, supply chains and production timelines. "The urgency is the name of the game," said Leonardo DRS President and CEO John Baylouny, adding that his company is purchasing materials and investing in production capacity before contracts are finalized to reduce delivery risk. Duffey describes AI-assisted development of F-47 One of the clearest illustrations of the Pentagon's evolving approach to acquisition came when Duffey described a recent visit to the Air Force office managing the F-47 next-generation fighter program in Dayton, OH. Rather than highlighting the aircraft itself, Duffey focused on how it is being developed. "I sat there and watched the engineer type natural language into an AI system that then piped out a code module that would then go into the F-47," Duffey said, describing a fully digital development environment in which government and industry engineers collaborate on the same platform. He said he believes the aircraft remains on track for a first flight before the current administration leaves office. For manufacturers, however, designing advanced systems is only part of the challenge. Chris Power, founder and CEO of Hadrian, argued that decades of offshoring have hollowed out the U.S. industrial base, making today's defense buildup "a once-in-50-year kind of event" requiring the nation to rebuild manufacturing capacity while simultaneously increasing weapons production. "You've got 40 years of causal sclerosis," Power said. "Now you're asking all of the heritage American companies to operate like SpaceX overnight." Power suggested future defense competitions could increasingly reward companies capable of producing systems quickly rather than those offering only marginal performance improvements. "A slightly degraded product that's ultra-producible" may prove more valuable than a more-capable system that cannot be manufactured at scale, he said, arguing a future global crisis may prompt the government to buy "whatever is there in the inventory at the time of need." Duffey called Power's remarks "words of wisdom," signaling the Pentagon shares industry's growing emphasis on manufacturing capacity as a competitive advantage. Power also argued workforce shortages -- not capital -- may become the biggest constraint on expanding production, saying the United States lacks enough skilled tradespeople to support the industrial expansion envisioned by both industry and the Pentagon, particularly in shipbuilding. That reality, he said, will require greater reliance on AI-enabled factories and digitally managed manufacturing. House Armed Services Committee Vice Chairman Rob Wittman (R-VA) said Congress shares the Pentagon's emphasis on accelerating procurement. "As we look at scale," Wittman said, "we also [have to do] that at the speed of relevance." He pointed to lessons emerging from Ukraine, describing recent operations in which unmanned aerial systems, ground robots and conventional forces operated together to seize and hold territory. China, he warned, is studying those developments closely. "We have to learn faster than they do," Wittman said. More multiyear deals coming Meanwhile, Jerry McGinn, a defense analyst at CSIS, said research indicates roughly 10,000 new firms have entered the defense market in the past two years, with DOD awarding new $120 billion in defense contracts to “new-entrants.” Additionally, McGinn highlighted DOD’s continued use of framework agreements and multiyear contracts to boost future munitions production. “I think Under Secretary Duffey’s arm is a little sore with all the long-term framework agreements that he’s been signing,” McGinn said. Duffey, meanwhile, said DOD wants to open up the multiyear contracting framework to other defense programs, eying the F-15 and F-35, focusing on “how can we create this certainty of demand when we know there’s this certainty of need.” “There’s a lot to be done but a lot to come,” he said.

title: Klara and the Sun Trailer: Ishiguro's AI Fiction Is Now Engineering Fact
description: Klara and the Sun trailer is now live: Taika Waititi's Sony Pictures adaptation of Ishiguro's novel hits theaters October 23, 2026, and every technology the book imagined is real. Vision Transformers process images as Klara does — in discrete patches. CRISPR germline editing has produced gene-edited humans. AI companion platforms face wrongful-death suits in federal court.
